does: This paper investigates whether neural networks trained on corrupted algorithmic datasets learn generalizable rules or memorize spurious patterns. Using modular arithmetic tasks with label corruption, the authors demonstrate that MLPs and Transformers can simultaneously achieve 100% generalization on clean data while memorizing corrupted labels. They develop methods to identify memorizing neurons through interpretable analysis of learned representations and show that pruning these neurons selectively reduces accuracy on corrupted examples while preserving or improving performance on clean data. The work provides concrete evidence that generalization and memorization operate through distinct neural mechanisms that can be analytically characterized and manipulated.
connects: This work extends the grokking literature initiated by Power et al., which observed sudden phase transitions from memorization to generalization in neural networks trained on algorithmic tasks. It builds on mechanistic interpretability research that seeks to understand learned computations at the neuron level, and connects to broader studies of the double descent phenomenon and implicit regularization in overparameterized networks. The paper responds to fundamental questions about robust generalization raised by adversarial robustness work and label-noise learning, particularly how networks distinguish signal from noise when training data is systematically corrupted. It complements concurrent work on neural collapse and feature learning that examines how different training phases encode different types of information.
enables: This research opens pathways for designing better defenses against label noise and adversarial corruption by targeting memorizing components directly. It suggests that mechanistic interpretability methods could identify and remove spurious learning in practical settings, potentially improving robustness without sacrificing clean accuracy. The framework of analytically tractable algorithmic tasks enables future work on understanding how architectural choices, optimization algorithms, and regularization schemes influence the memorization-generalization tradeoff. Results could inform development of pruning and regularization techniques that surgically preserve generalizable knowledge while eliminating brittle memorization, with applications to noisy real-world datasets and contaminated training sets in safety-critical domains.
